## Definition

**Carbohydrates** is introduced in this chapter. # 5 Carbohydrates

Carbohydrates are one of the major energy sources for the human body, alongside fats and proteins. They are composed of chemical elements that, together with water, vitamins and minerals, are essential to sustain healthy living.

This chapter covers the role of carbohydrates in sport and exercise, the structure of monosaccharides (glucose and its isomers, fructose), the formation of disaccharides via glycoside bonds, variations in polysaccharides, the structure of glycogen granules, and the metabolic processes of glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis and glycogenesis. It also addresses the formation and fates of lactic acid, the link reaction and TCA cycle, electron transfer and oxidative phosphorylation, and calculation of ATP yield from glucose breakdown via anaerobic and aerobic systems.

## Core Principles

Classifier signals: section_heading, freq_multi_section.

Carbohydrates, as energy sources, are built from chemical elements that are fundamental components of the human body. An overview of the main chemical elements within the human body is provided in foundational biochemistry references (e.g., Table 3.1 in the source chapter).

Carbohydrate metabolism in sport and exercise relies on key biochemical processes: glycogenolysis (breakdown of glycogen), glycolysis (breakdown of glucose), gluconeogenesis (synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors), and glycogenesis (synthesis of glycogen). Energy extraction from glucose proceeds via the link reaction, the TCA cycle, electron transfer, and oxidative phosphorylation, yielding ATP through both anaerobic and aerobic pathways. Lactic acid is formed and further metabolised as part of these pathways.

- ATP yield from anaerobic and aerobic glucose breakdown

## Practical Applications

Carbohydrates serve as one of the primary energy sources sustaining human life and healthy living.

In sport and exercise contexts, carbohydrates provide ATP through both anaerobic and aerobic pathways, with the relative contribution depending on exercise intensity and duration. Glycogen stores act as a key fuel reserve, and the regulation of glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis and glycogenesis underpins fuel availability during and after exercise.
